title: Asymptotic expansion of solutions to Markov renewal equations
text:
We consider the Markov renewal equation \(F(t) = f(t) + \boldsymbol{\mu}*F(t)\) for vector-valued functions \(f,F: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}^{p}\) and a \(p \times p\) matrix \(\boldsymbol{\mu}\) of locally finite measures \(\mu^{i,j}\) on \([0,\infty)\), \(i,j=1,\ldots,p\). In the talk, I will talk about a new, more elementary proof proof of an asymptotic expansion for the solution \(F\) to the above equation that goes back originally to Sgibnev [1]. I will address its application in the theory of multi-type general branching processes with finite type space.
Bibliography
\([1]\) Sgibnev, M. S. Semimultiplicative estimates for the solution of the multidimensional renewal equation. Izvestiya: Mathematics, 66(3), 595-610, 2002

title: Average-weight percolation on the complete graph
text:
This talk is based on a joint work with Elie Aïdékon (Fudan University, China).
Attach to each edge of the complete graph on \(n\) vertices, i.i.d. exponential random variables with mean \(n\). Aldous (1998) proved that the longest path with average weight below \(p\) undergoes a phase transition at \(p=\frac{1}{e}\): it is \(o(n)\) when \(p<\frac{1}{e}\) and of order \(n\) if \(p>\frac1e\). Later, Ding (2013) revealed a finer phase transition around \(\frac{1}{e}\): there exist \(c'>c>0\) such that the length of the longest path is of order \(\ln^3 n\) if \(p \le \frac{1}{e}+\frac{c}{\ln^2 n}\) and is polynomial if \(p\ge  \frac{1}{e}+\frac{c'}{\ln^2 n}\). We identify the location of this phase transition and obtain sharp asymptotics of the length near criticality. The proof uses an exploration mechanism mimicking a branching random walk with selection introduced by Brunet and Derrida (1999).
Bibliography
\([1]\) Aldous, D. (1998). On the critical value for ‘percolation’ of minimum-weight trees in the mean-field distance model. Comb. Probab. Comput., 7, 1–10.
\([2]\) Bérard, J. and Gouéré, J.-B.  (2010). Brunet-Derrida behavior of branching-selection particle systems on the line. Commun. Math. Phys., 298, 323–342.
\([3]\) Brunet, E. and Derrida, B.  (1999). Microscopic models of traveling wave equations. Computer Physics Communications, 121-122, 376–381.
\([4]\) Ding, J.  (2013). Scaling window for mean-field percolation of averages. Ann. Probab. 41, 4407–4427.
\([5]\) Ding, J. and Goswami, S.  (2015). Percolation of averages in the stochastic mean field model: the near-supercritical regime. Electron. J. Probab., 20.

title: Can one hear the shape of a random matrix?
text:
Homepage 

Consider a random matrix \(X\) whose entries are i.i.d. in the cells of a Young diagram (its ‘shape’) and zero elsewhere. When the shape is the dilation by a factor \(N\) of a fixed Young diagram \(\lambda\), the Wishart-type matrix \(XX^*\) (suitably rescaled) has, as \(N\to\infty\), a limiting spectral distribution \(F^{\lambda}\) characterised by its moments. These moments enumerate \(\lambda\)-plane trees, a class of directed plane trees with vertex labelling compatible with \(\lambda\), for which we provide explicit enumerative formulae. We show that one cannot ‘hear the shape of a random matrix’, in the sense that there exist distinct Young diagrams yielding the same limiting spectral distribution. We establish that the classes of ‘isospectral’ Young diagrams are those with the same diagonal profile.
Based on the article in preparation \([1]\).
Bibliography
\([1]\) Elia Bisi, Fabio D. Cunden, Ivailo Hartarsky, Stephan Wagner. “Can one hear the shape of a random matrix?”. In preparation, 2026+.

title: Mass erasure of infinitely measured Polish R-trees, and applications to Lévy forests
text:
Website 

In this talk we will discuss a larger ongoing project (based on the core theory introduced in an upcoming paper by Duquesne and Winkel \([4]\)), developing the theory of mass erasure. The main difficulty this theory addresses is introducing a natural framework in which one may study convergence of finitely \([4]\) / infinitely \([5a]\) measured Polish \(\mathbb{R}\)-trees which are not locally compact. Such \(\mathbb{R}\)-trees may be thought of as containing large ‘hubs’, either in the form of infinite-degree branch points or accumulations of branch points, in which infinitely many of the subtrees featured are taller than some fixed size, say \(\varepsilon > 0\).



An illustration of how local compactness (or the lack thereof) may manifest itself in a Polish ℝ-tree. Here, in the form of an infinite-degree branch point, in which the subtrees have either fixed height (not locally compact) or vanishing height (locally compact).


Mass erasure provides a technique for trimming such \(\mathbb{R}\)-trees in a similar way to the trimming or leaf-length erasure known from e.g., \([8]\) or \([3]\), but where subtrees are erased according to their mass rather than their height with respect to some erasure parameter \(h > 0\) (controlling how much mass is erased, with the tree being fully recovered as \(h \searrow 0\)). This allows for the extraction of simpler discrete subtrees, which may easily be studied using the classical Gromov-type topologies. Considering mass erasure across multiple parameters \(h > 0\) tending to zero, we may construct new topologies of (weak/vague) convergence in the sense of mass erasure.
We will in particular focus on how the theory of mass erasure may be applied to obtain invariance principles for Galton-Watson forests in a broader setting than what has been done in existing literature \([2,3]\). Namely, we will look at how to construct Lévy forests when the so-called Grey’s condition fails, using convergence in the sense of mass erasure, thus providing in full generality an analogous result to \([6]\) in the setting of random trees. The below table provides an overview of the different settings in which Lévy forests may be constructed as scaling limits of Galton-Watson forests, with the mode of convergence and assumptions being the ‘strongest’ in the upper-left corner and the ‘weakest’ in the lower-right corner.




(Sub-)critical
Supercritical




Grey
Duquesne and Le Gall \([2]\)
Duquesne and Winkel \([3]\)



(Height functions \(\Rightarrow\) Gromov-Hausdorff)
(Local Gromov-Hausdorff)


No Grey
Duquesne and Winkel \([4]\)
G. \([5b]\)



(Finite mass erasure)
(Infinite mass erasure)



If time permits, we may also discuss some newer ongoing work aiming to characterize finitely measured Polish \(\mathbb{R}\)-trees using coding-functions, in a similar manner to how height functions were used to encode and study the convergence of compact \(\mathbb{R}\)-trees in \([2]\).
Bibliography
\([1]\) S. Athreya, W. Löhr, A. Winter (2016). The gap between Gromov-vague and Gromov–Hausdorff-vague topology. Stochastic Processes and Their Applications, Vol. 126, pp. 2527-2553.
\([2]\) T. Duquesne, J.F. Le Gall (2002). Random Trees, Lévy Processes and Spatial Branching Processes. Astérisque No. 281.
\([3]\) T. Duquesne, M. Winkel (2019). Hereditary Tree Growth and Lévy Forests. Stochastic Processes and their Applications, Vol. 129(10), pp. 3690-3747.
\([4]\) T. Duquesne, M. Winkel (2026+). Mass Erasure on Measured \(\mathbb{R}\)-Trees and Applications to Lévy Forests. In preparation.
\([5a]\) M. Glückstad (2026+). Mass erasure of infinitely measured Polish \(\mathbb{R}\)-trees I: Construction, continuity, and convergence. In preparation.
\([5b]\) M. Glückstad (2026+). Mass erasure of infinitely measured Polish \(\mathbb{R}\)-trees II: Applications to supercritical Galton-Watson and Lévy forests. In preparation.
\([6]\) A. Grimvall (1974). On the convergence of a sequence of branching processes. Annals of Probability, Vol. 2(1974), pp. 1027–1045.
\([7]\) J.F. Le Gall, Y. Le Jan (1998). Branching processes in Lévy processes: The exploration process. Annals of Probability, Vol. 26(1), pp. 213-252.
\([8]\) J. Neveu (1986). Erasing a branching tree. Advances in Applied Probability, Vol. 18, pp. 101-108.

title: On the tails of Dickman-like perpetuities
text:
Homepage 

Let \((M_k, Q_k)_{k\geq 1}\) be independent copies of an \(\mathbb{R}^2\)-valued random vector \((M,Q)\) defined on a common probability space. Whenever a random series \[Q_1+\sum_{k\geq 1}M_1\cdot\ldots\cdot M_kQ_{k+1}\] converges almost surely, its sum that we denote by \(Z\) is called perpetuity.
In Theorem 3.1(a) of \([1]\) it is shown that if \(|Z|<\infty\) a.s., \(M\) and \(Q\) are independent, \(\mathbb{P}\{M\in [0,1]\}=1\), the function \(\delta \mapsto \delta^{-1}\mathbb{P}\{M\in [1-\delta, 1]\}\) is bounded away from \(0\) and \(\infty\) for small positive \(\delta\) and the constant \(b:=\sup\{x: \mathbb{P}\{Q\geq x\}>0\}\) satisfies \(b\in (0,\infty)\), then \[\begin{equation*}
-\log \mathbb{P}\{Z>t\}~\sim~ b^{-1}t\log t,\quad t\to\infty,
\end{equation*}\] that is, the right distribution tail of \(Z\) exhibits a Poisson-like decay.
We became curious whether a precise, rather than logarithmic, asymptotic can be derived under comparable assumptions. Theorem below answers this question in the affirmative for Dickman-like perpetuities, which are defined by:

\(M\) and \(Q\) are independent;
\(M\) has a beta distribution with parameters \(\alpha>0\) and \(1\), that is, \(\mathbb{P}\{M\in{\rm d}x\}=\alpha x^{\alpha-1}{\rm 1}_{(0,1)}(x){\rm d}x\);
\(\mathbb{E} [\log^+|Q|]<\infty\) and
\(b\in (0,\infty)\).

Under the standing assumptions, the distribution of \(Z\) is absolutely continuous with respect to Lebesgue measure. Let \(p\) denote the corresponding density. For each \(t>0\), the equation \(\alpha s^{-1}(\mathbb{E}[{\rm e}^{sQ}]-1)=t\) has a unique positive solution that we denote by \(s(t)\).
Theorem. Under the standing assumptions, as \(t\to\infty\), \[p(t)~\sim~\frac{1}{\alpha (2\pi b)^{1/2}}t^{1/2}s(t) \exp\Big(-ts(t)+\alpha \int_0^{s(t)}\frac{g(y)-1}{y}{\rm d}y\Big)\] and \[\mathbb{P}\{Z>t\}~\sim~\frac{p(t)}{s(t)}.\]
Our proof is purely probabilistic. It is based on the exponential change of measure. Other approaches were used in [2,3] to address a similar problem for another subclass of perpetuities given by \(\int_0^\infty {\rm e}^{-S(t)}{\rm d}t\), where \((S(t))_{t\geq 0}\) is a subordinator.
The talk is based on work in progress joint with Oleh Iksanov. The work reported in the talk was supported by the National Research Foundation of Ukraine (project 2023.03/0059 ‘Contribution to modern theory of random series’).
Bibliography
\([1]\) C. M. Goldie and R. Grübel. "Perpetuities with thin tails." Adv. Appl. Probab., 28, 1996, pp.  463–480.
\([2]\) B. Haas. "Precise asymptotics for the density and the upper tail of exponential functionals of subordinators." Séminaire de Probabilités LII, Lecture Notes in Math. 2363, 2025, pp. 389–426.
\([3]\) M. Minchev and M. Savov. "Asymptotics for densities of exponential functionals of subordinators." Bernoulli. 29, 2023, pp.  3307–3333.

title: Growing self-similar Markov trees
text:
Can we obtain a Brownian CRT of mass \(1/2\) from a CRT of mass \(1\) by cutting certain branches?
In this talk, I will answer that question in the much more general setting of self-similar Markov trees. Self-similar Markov trees (ssMt) are random decorated trees that encode the genealogy of a system of particles carrying positive labels, and where particles undergo splitting and growth depending on their labels in a self-similar fashion. Introduced and developed in the recent monograph \([1]\), they provide a broad generalization of Brownian and stable continuum random trees and arise naturally in various models of random geometry such as the Brownian sphere/disk. The law of a ssMt is characterized by its quadruplet \((\mathrm{a}, \sigma^2, \boldsymbol{\Xi}; \alpha)\), which specifies the features of the underlying growth–fragmentation mechanism, together with the initial decoration \(x>0\).
In this presentation, I focus on special cases of ssMt in which the trees started from different initial values \(x>0\) can be coupled into a continuous, increasing family of nested subtrees. In the case of the Brownian and stable continuum random trees, this yields surprisingly simple novel dynamics corresponding to the scaling limit of the leaf-growth algorithms of Łuczak–Winkler and Caraceni–Stauffer.
Based on joint work with Nicolas Curien and William Fleurat.
Theorem. If the characteristic quadruplet \(( \mathrm{a}, \sigma^2, \boldsymbol{\Xi} ; \alpha)\) of a ssMt is growing, then we can on the same probability space construct a family \(\mathtt{T}_x = (T_x, d_{T_x}, \rho_x, g_x)\) for \(x > 0\) of decorated random trees so that
Law. For any \(x > 0\) the decorated tree \(\mathtt{T}_x\) has law \(\mathbb{Q}_x\).
Monotonicity. The application \(x \mapsto \mathtt{T}_x\) is non-decreasing for the inclusion, i.e. \(\mathtt{T}_x \subset \mathtt{T}_{x + h}\).
Continuity. The application \(x \mapsto \mathtt{T}_x\) is continuous.
Markov forward. The decorated tree \(\mathtt{T}_x\) comes with a sequence of points \(p_x(i) \in T_x\) for \(i \geq 1\). For \(x'<x\), there are weights \(w_{x' \to x}(i)>0\) such that \(T_x\) is obtained from \(T_{x'}\) by gluing independent ssMt of initial decoration \(w_{x' \to x}(i)\) onto \(p_{x'}(i)\).

 

A family of coupled Brownian CRTs with masses 0, 0.3, 0.6, 1 respectively.


Bibliography
\([1]\) J. Bertoin, N. Curien, and A. Riera. “Self-similar Markov trees and scaling limits”. arXiv preprint arXiv:2407.07888 (2024). URL: https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.07888

title: On a population model with memory
text:
Homepage 

Consider first a memoryless population model described by the usual branching process with a given mean reproduction matrix on a finite space of types. Motivated by the consequences of atavism in Evolutionary Biology, we are interested in a modification of the dynamics where individuals keep full memory of their forebears and procreation involves the reactivation of a gene picked at random on the ancestral lineage. By comparing the spectral radii of the two mean reproduction matrices (with and without memory), we observe that, on average, the model with memory always grows at least as fast as the model without memory. The proof relies on analyzing a biased Markov chain on the space of memories. The existence of a unique ergodic law for the latter is demonstrated through asymptotic coupling.
Bibliography
\([1]\) Bertoin, J. On a population model with memory. Bernoulli (to appear)

title: Growth of CMJ processes without Malthusian parameter
text: It is well-known that the number \(N(t)\) of births up to time \(t \geq 0\) in a CMJ process grows asymptotically like \(\mathrm{e}^{\alpha t}\) where \(\alpha > 0\) is the abscissa where the Laplace transform \(m\) of the intensity measure \(\mu\) of the reproduction point process hits the level \(1\). We consider the situation where \(m(1) < 1\) and \(m(\theta) = \infty\) for all \(\theta < 1\). We prove that under certain assumptions (including Poisson reproduction) we obtain that \(N(t)\) grows like \(\mathrm{e}^{t} F(t,t+1]\) where \(F(\mathrm{d} x) = \mathrm{e}^{-x}\mu(\mathrm{d} x)\) is the tilted intensity measure.

title: Scale invariance beyond self-similarity
text:
Homepage 

In this talk, we discuss several forms of scale invariance that extend the classical notion of self-similarity. First, we consider stochastic self-similarity (multifractality) of stochastic processes based on scaling of finite-dimensional distributions. We show some general properties of these processes and introduce new examples inspired by the idea of Lamperti transformation.
We then focus on scale invariance properties of non-negative integer-valued processes. These can be defined by replacing standard multiplication with the thinning operation corresponding to a continuous-time Markov branching process. We extend this to random factors and define stochastic self-similarity for integer-valued processes. The main class of examples is provided by time-changed compound Poisson processes. Finally, we will briefly discuss ongoing work on analogous notions for point processes.
This research is partly motivated by the so-called Taylor’s law, an empirical phenomenon describing a power-law relationship between sample mean and variance.

title: Explosive Crump–Mode–Jagers branching processes: An infinite star or path, that is the question
text:
Homepage 

We study a general class of Crump–Mode–Jagers branching processes. Initialised with a single ancestor at time \(0\), every individual that is born produces children according to an i.i.d. copy of a point process \(\xi\) on \(\mathbb R_+\). The points of \(\xi\) denote the birth-times of the children of an individual (with respect to the birth-time of their parent). We write \[\xi=\sum_{k=1}^{D} \delta_{\sum_{i=1}^k X_i},\] where \(D\) and \(X_i\) denote the number of children of an individual and the inter-birth time between child \(i-1\) and child \(i\), respectively. We say the branching process explodes when it grows infinitely large in finite time with positive probability. The occurrence of explosion has (mostly) been studied when either \(D<\infty\) almost surely or \(D=\infty\) almost surely. In this talk, we ‘fill the gap’ by considering the case \(\mathbb P(D=\infty)\in(0,1)\). Under the additional assumption that \[\sum_{i=1}^\infty X_i<\infty \text{ almost surely},\] the branching process almost surely grows infinitely large in finite time conditionally on survival. We investigate how the branching process explodes in two regimes, classified by \(\mathbb E[D 1_{\{D<\infty\}}]<\infty\) and \(\mathbb E[D 1_{\{D<\infty\}}]=\infty\). We provide sufficient conditions under which explosion is caused, in the former regime, by a unique individual with infinite offspring, and in the latter regime, by a unique infinite path of individuals whilst all individuals have produced only finite offspring.
Joint work with Markus Heydenreich.

title: Towards central limit theorem of critical branching random walks
text:
Homepage 

We present recent progress on the central limit theorem for the range of a critical branching random walk (CBRW) in \(\mathbb Z^d\) conditioned to be infinite. Law of large number for range of CBRW was given by Le Gall and Lin \([1]\) in 2016, and we aim at strengthening the result to a central limit theorem, based on general theory for stationary process \([2]\) and relation between height process of CBRW and random walk excursions. This talk is based on a joint work with Yueyun Hu.
Bibliography
\([1]\) Le Gall, J.-F., Lin, S.: The range of tree-indexed random walk. J. Inst. Math. Jussieu 15(2), 271–317 (2016).
\([2]\) Dedecker, J., Merlevède, F.: Necessary and sufficient conditions for the condi- tional central limit theorem. Ann. Probab. 30(3), 1044–1081 (2002).

title: Biased random walk on the critical curve of dynamical percolation
text:
We study biased random walks on dynamical percolation in \(\mathbb{Z}^d\), which were introduced by Andres et al. in \([1]\). We provide a second order expansion for the asymptotic speed as a function of the bias for \(d=1\) and show for \(d\geq 2\) that the speed of the biased random walk on the critical curve is monotone increasing for large values of the bias.
Our methods are based on studying the environment seen from the walker as well as a combination of ergodicity and couplings arguments.
Joint work with Dominik Schmid.
Bibliography
\([1]\) Sebastian Andres, Nina Gantert, Dominik Schmid, and Perla Sousi. "Biased random walk on dynamical percolation." The Annals of Probability, vol. 52, no. 6, 2024, pp. 2051-2078. 10.1214/23-AOP1679

title: The limit behaviour of some phylogenetic tree balance indices
text:
GitHub   Homepage 

Balance indices for phylogenetic trees, like Colless’s \([8]\) Sackin’s \([12]\), the cophenetic index \([10]\) or the quartet index (QI \([9]\)) admit a recursive representation that allows for finding an explicit representation of their (after scaling and centring) limit in distribution through the so–called contraction method. This method was particularly successful in studying the weak limit of the normalized number of comparisons in the Quicksort algorithm \([11]\).
Commonly the weak limits of the (scaled and centred) indices were studied for pure–birth or uniform models of tree growth (e.g. \([3,6,7]\)). However, in \([9]\) the QI was considered for much wider class of phylogenetic tree models–Aldous’s \(\beta\) model \([1,2]\). In \([4]\) we showed that the contraction method can be successfully used to characterize the weak–limit of a much wide class of indices under Aldous’s \(\beta\ge 0\) model. Namely, if for a rooted binary tree with \(n\) leaves, a normalized index, \(Y_{n}\), satisfies the recursive equation \[Y_{n} = \left(\frac{L_{n}}{n} \right)^{r} Y_{L\_{n}} + \left(1-\frac{L_{n}}{n} \right)^{r} Y_{n-L_{n}}
+C_{n}(L_{n}),\] where \(L_{n}\) is the leaf clade size of one of the two daughter clades of the root, and \[C_{n}(i)=n^{-r}\left(\sum\limits_{r_{1}+r_{2}+r_{3}}C_{r_{1},r_{2},r_{3}}i^{r_{i}}(n-i)^{r_{2}}n^{r_{3}}+h_{n}(i)
\right)\] is such that under our model \(\mathrm{E}[C_{n}(L_{n})]=0\), and \(\sup_{i}n^{r}h_{n}(i)\to 0\) and \(\mathrm{E}[Y_{n}^{2}]\) is uniformly bounded, then \(Y_{n}\) converges in the Wasserstein \(d\)–metric, to a random variable whose distribution is the unique fixed point of a particular transformation.
In the talk we will discuss the above result, its limit and examples of phylogenetic tree indices that fall into this framework (e.g., QI\(^{2}\) \([5]\)). The results will be illustrated with simulations.
Acknowledgments KB’s research is supported by the Swedish Research Council’s (Vetenskapsrådet) grants no. \(2017\)–\(04951\), \(2025\)–\(03823\), and an ELLIIT Call C grant.
Bibliography
\([1]\) D. Aldous, Probability distributions on cladograms. Random Discrete Structures, Springer–Verlag (1996), 1–18.
\([2]\) D. Aldous, Stochastic models and descriptive statistics for phylogenetic trees. Statistical Science 16 (2001), 23–34.
\([3]\) K. Bartoszek, Exact and approximate limit behaviour of the Yule tree’s cophenetic index. Mathematical Biosciences 303 (2018), 26–45.
\([4]\) K. Bartoszek, Limit distribution of the quartet balance index for Aldous’s \(\\beta \\ge 0\)–model. Applicationes Mathematicae 47 (2020), 29-44.
\([5]\) K. Bartoszek, T. M. Coronado, A. Mir and F. Rosselló, Squaring within the Colless index yields a better balance index. Mathematical Biosciences 331 (2021), 108503.
\([6]\) M. G. B. Blum and O. François, On statistical tests of phylogenetic tree imbalance: The Sackin and other indices revisited. Mathematical Biosciences 195 (2005), 141–153.
\([7]\) M. G. B. Blum, O. François and S. Janson, The mean, variance and limiting distribution of two statistics sensitive to phylogenetic tree balance. Annals of Applied Probability 16 (2006), 2195–2214.
\([8]\) D. H. Colless, Review of “Phylogenetics: the theory and practice of phylogenetic systematics”. Systematic Zoology 31 (1982), 100–104.
\([9]\) T. Martínez–Coronado, A. Mir, F. Rosselló and G. Valiente, A balance index for phylogenetic trees based on quartets. Journal of Mathematical Biology 79 (2019), 1105–1148.
\([10]\) A. Mir, F. Rosselló and L. Rotger, A new balance index for phylogenetic trees. Mathematical Biosciences 241 (2013), 125–136.
\([11]\) U. Rösler, A limit theorem for “Quicksort”. Theoretical Informatics and Applications—Informatique Théorique et Applications, 25 (1991), 85–100.
\([12]\) M. J. Sackin, “Good” and “bad” phenograms. Systematic Zoology 21 (1972), 225–226.

title: A recent development in a random walk with dependent steps and its limiting process
text:
Homepage 

Recently, a generalized Bernoulli process (GBP) was developed, which is a stationary binary sequence that can have long-range dependence. The large-scale behavior of GBP resembles that of the fractional Poisson process, as both, when scaled, have the same limiting distribution. GBP was extended to include various covariance functions, and the limiting process of a random walk that follows GBP was found, called the Exponential Mittag-Leffler (EML) process. The EML process is self-similar and has stationary increments whose correlation decays with an exponential rate, power-law, or tempered power-law. The marginal density of the EML process solves a time tempered fractional diffusion equation. When Lévy processes are subordinated to the EML process, it results in various non-Markovian diffusion processes that are governed by space–time tempered fractional diffusion equations.
Bibliography
\([1]\) Lee, J. (2021). Generalized Bernoulli process with long-range dependence and fractional binomial distribution. Dependence Modeling, 9(1), 1-12. https://doi.org/10.1515/demo-2021-0100
\([2]\) Lee, J. (2025). Generalized Bernoulli process and fractional Poisson process. Stochastics, 97(4), 539–570. https://doi.org/10.1080/17442508.2025.2498386
\([3]\) Lee, J. (2026). Scaling limit of dependent random walk. Chaos, Solitons & Fractals, 208, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chaos.2026.118102.

title: Recent progress on Bernoulli-Laplace urn models
text:
Homepage 

The Bernoulli-Laplace urn is a classical example of a Markov chain, which can be used to model diffusion. We will focus on its mixing time, which quantifies the speed of convergence to the stationary distribution. In 1981, Diaconis and Shahshahani showed that the Bernoulli-Laplace urn exhibits cutoff: A sharp transition from unmixed to mixed. In this talk, we study the limit profile, capturing the exact way of the transition from unmixed to mixed [2]. Moreover, we investigate the occurrence of the cutoff phenomenon for a multi-urn and multi-color version of the Bernoulli-Laplace urn model [1]. This talk is based on joint works with Sam Olesker-Taylor, and with Ritesh Goenka and Jonathan Hermon.
Bibliography
\([1]\) Cutoff for generalised Bernoulli-Laplace urn models, Ritesh Goenka, Jonathan Hermon, and Dominik Schmid. Available at arXiv
\([2]\) Limit profile for the Bernoulli-Laplace urn, Sam Olesker-Taylor and Dominik Schmid, to appear at Annals of Applied Probability. Available at arXiv

title: Annihilating branching Brownian motion
text:
Homepage 

Place positive/negative particles at \(\pm1\) along the real line. From each point, sets of signed particles attempt to grow according to branching Brownian motion, but when opposing particles meet, their signs cancel and the particles annihilate. We show that coexistence is possible and that, on this event, the interface is asymptotically linear in time, with a random slope. Several open problems will be discussed. Joint work with Daniel Ahlberg (Stockholm University) and Omer Angel (University of British Columbia).
Bibliography
\([1]\) D. Ahlberg, O. Angel, and B. Kolesnik. Annihilating branching Brownian motion. Int. Math. Res. Not., no. 13, 2024, pp. 10425–10448. https://doi.org/10.1093/imrn/rnae068

title: Limit shape of the leaky Abelian sandpile model
text:
Website 

The Abelian sandpile model is a model from physics that emerged in the 1980s to illustrate the phenomenon of self-organized criticality. It is a cellular automaton that simulates the unstable accumulation of sand grains, followed by topples that eventually lead to a stable pile. A fractal structure can be observed in the resulting configurations. In this talk, we focus on the shape of the boundary of the final configuration as the number of sand grains tends to infinity. In the classical model, many conjectures remain open. We instead study a variant called the “leaky” model, in which a portion of the sand disappears after each toppling.
Generalizing the work of Alevy and Mkrtchyan \([1]\), who considered a particular case in the plane, we prove a convergence result toward a limiting shape in arbitrary dimension and for a broad class of toppling rules. We then investigate the influence of the leakiness parameter, which is the distinctive feature of this model. Although the model under consideration is deterministic, the description of the limiting shape relies on probabilistic tools involving the Green function of a killed Markov chain.
This is joint work with Théo Ballu, Cédric Boutillier, and Sevak Mkrtchyan \([2]\). A short video (in French) from the “5 minutes Lebesgue” series, in which the first author, Théo Ballu, presents an overview of this work, is available


Bibliography
\([1]\) Ian Alevy and Sevak Mkrtchyan. “The limit shape of the leaky abelian sandpile model.” Int. Math. Res. Not., vol. 16, pp. 12767–12802. doi
\([2]\) Théo Ballu, Cédric Boutillier, Sevak Mkrtchyan and Kilian Raschel. “Limit shape of the leaky Abelian sandpile model with multiple layers.” Preprint, arXiv:2501.12664

title: Generalized gamma convolution laws from continuous branching processes
text:
Homepage 

In this joint work with Anita Behme, we prove that the limit of the Heyde martingale in supercritical, continuous-time, and continuous-state branching processes is often a generalized Gamma convolution. This provides an answer to an open problem raised by Anthony Pakes.
Bibliography
\([1]\) Bertoin, J. (1996). Lévy processes. Cambridge University Press, 1996.
\([2]\) David R. Grey. "Asymptotic behaviour of continuous-time continuous state-space branching processes". J. Appl. Probab. vol. 11, 1974, pp. 669–677. doi: 10.2307/321255
\([3]\) Anthony G. Pakes. "Self-Decomposable laws from continuous branching processes". J. Theor. Probab. vol. 33, 2020, pp. 361–395. doi: s10959-019-00886-0
\([4]\) Schilling, R. L.; Song, R; and Vondraček, Z. Bernstein Functions. Theory and applications, second edition, de Gruyter Studies in Mathematics, 37. Walter de Gruyter & Co., Berlin, 2012.

title: Random walks with square-root boundaries
text:
Let \(S(n)\) be a real valued random walk with i.i.d. increments which have zero mean and finite variance. We are interested in the asymptotic properties of the stopping time \(T(g):=\inf\{n\ge1: S(n)\le g(n)\}\), where \(g(t)\) is a boundary function. We first consider the parametric family of boundaries \(\{g_{a,b}(t)=c\sqrt{t+b}-a, b\ge0, a>c\sqrt{b}\}\). Assuming that sufficiently many moments of increments of the walk are finite, we construct a positive space-time harmonic function \(W(a,b)\). Then we show that there exist \(p(c)>0\) and a constant \(\varkappa(c)\) such that \[\mathbb{P}(T_{g_{a,b}}>n)\sim \varkappa(c)\frac{W(a,b)}{n^{p(c)/2}}
\quad\text{ as }n\to\infty.\] We also discuss the tail behaviour of \(T(g)\) under a weaker assumption on the boundary. Namely, we just assume that \(g(t)\sim c\sqrt{t}\) as \(t\to\infty\).

title: Metric Explorations of Random Surfaces
text:
Homepage 

This talk will introduce several classes of discrete random surface models and their continuous scaling limits.
We will then describe how these limiting surfaces can be analyzed using branching processes and self-similar Markov processes. In particular, we will see that these processes provide a natural way to define metric explorations of continuous random surfaces, including those that arise in Brownian geometry as scaling limits of large generic graphs embedded on surfaces.
No particular prerequisites are required. The focus will be on the key ideas and methods behind the study of these objects, rather than on technical details.

title: Upper moderate deviations of the maximum of a branching random walk
text:
Consider \(M_n\) the maximal position at generation \(n\) of a supercritical branching random walk. In 2013, Elie Aïdékon \([1]\) obtained and described the convergence in law, as \(n\) goes to infinity, of \(M_n-m_n\), where \(m_n\) is an explicit function. Equivalently, he identified the limit of \(\mathbb{P}(M_n > m_n + x)\), for any \(x \in \mathbb{R}\). More recently, Lianghui Luo \([3]\) gave an asymptotic equivalent for the upper large deviation probability, that is \(\mathbb{P}(M_n > m_n + xn)\), for \(x > 0\). In this talk, I will present a joint work with Lianghui Luo (IMT - Université de Toulouse) in which we study an intermediate regime, called moderate. Specifically, we obtain an asymptotic equivalent for \(\mathbb{P}(M_n > m_n + x_n)\), where \(x_n\) is such that \(x_n \to \infty\) and \(x_n = O(\sqrt{n})\). As a byproduct, we obtain information about the typical behavior of particles contributing to such deviations.
Bibliography
\([1]\) Aïdékon, E. (2013). Convergence in law of the minimum of a branching random walk. Ann. Probab., 41(3A), 1362–1426.
\([2]\) Bramson, M., Ding, J. and Zeitouni, O. (2016). Convergence in law of the maximum of nonlattice branching random walk. Ann. Inst. Henri Poincaré Probab. Stat., 52(4), 1897–1924.
\([3]\) Luo, L. (2025). Precise upper deviation estimates for the maximum of a branching random walk. Electron. J. Probab., 30, Paper No. 18, 30.

title: Local limit of directed animals on the square lattice
text:
Homepage 

Directed animals on the square lattice, counted by volume, were first enumerated by Dhar using a duality with hard-core lattice gas models \([1]\), and later by Gouyou-Beauchamps and Viennot through a path representation and the theory of heaps of pieces \([2]\).
In this talk, we study the local limit of directed animals viewed from their root.
We present three constructions of the limiting object: first, as a heap of pieces whose coordinates evolve according to a modified random walk on the integers; second, layer by layer, as a Markov chain with an explicit transition kernel and long-range interactions; and third, as a branching-annihilating particle system in which the interactions between particles are mediated by intertwined "antiparticles".
We also look at geometric properties of the local limit and establish, in particular, that it consists in a single vertex at infinitely many (random) levels.
Joint work with Édouard Maurel-Ségala and Arvind Singh (IMO, Université Paris-Saclay), based partly on \([3]\).
Bibliography
\([1]\) Dhar, D. (1982). “Equivalence of the two-dimensional directed-site animal problem to Baxter’s hard-square lattice-gas model.” Physical Review Letters, 49(14), 959.
\([2]\) Gouyou-Beauchamps, D. and Viennot, G. X. (1988). “Equivalence of the two-dimensional directed animal problem to a one-dimensional path problem.” Advances in Applied Mathematics, 9(3), 334–357.
\([3]\) Hénard, O., Maurel-Ségala, É. and Singh, A. (2024). “The local limit of rooted directed animals on the square lattice.” Probability Theory and Related Fields, 1–64.

title: The Integrable Snake Model
text:
Website 

On the integer lattice \(\mathbb{Z}^2\), a "pure snake configuration" is a permutation on the vertices containing no two-cycles and such that every vertex is mapped to either itself, the vertex to the right, the vertex above or the vertex below. As such, a pure snake configuration admits an interpretation as a collection of snaking, non-intersecting paths on \(\mathbb{Z}^2\). Pure snake configurations are a generalisation of lozenge tilings, which are in natural correspondence with paths that only travel right or up. We consider a partition function on a finite version of this model and see the probabilistic properties of random pure snake configurations chosen according to their contribution to this partition function. Under a suitable weighting, the model is integrable in the sense that we have access to explicit formulas for its partition function and correlation function. We will note the integrable and determinantal structure of this model, even through its various scaling limits, and realise a traffic representation of ASEP on the ring.

title: Abstract example
text:
GitHub   Website 

This is a sample abstract to showcase the flexibility of the HTML format. We still support LaTeX commands \[\begin{equation*}
    \int_\mathbb{R} f(x) \mathrm{d} x = \lim_{n \to \infty} \sum_{k} f(x_k) \Delta x_k.
\end{equation*}\]
Theorem. Theorems are stated as such, for example \[\begin{equation*}
    1+1=2
\end{equation*}\]
